data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Climate-driven subsistence crisis cascaded through networked palace economies whose interdependence amplified local failures into systemic collapse", "mechanism": "Severe Mediterranean drought 1250-1150 BC (palynological and Dead Sea level evidence) reduced cereal yields; palace states with centralized storage and seed-fund redistribution could not absorb multi-year shortfalls because surplus extraction had already peaked under demographic pressure; Hittite grain imports from Egypt/Ugarit failed simultaneously, triggering military mobilization for survival resources that further destabilized trade networks; Egypt survived because Nile flood regime buffered it from Levantine drought patterns and its temple-based storage permitted fragmented local adaptation", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egyptian survival"], "excludes": ["Mesopotamian polities", "Iranian plateau", "Egyptian 20th Dynasty contraction"] }, "forbidden": [{"case": "Palace archives show abundant grain reserves and normal price stability at Ugarit until its destruction horizon", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian agricultural system remained palace-dependent with no temple autonomy during the 19th-20th Dynasties", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite collapse preceded drought evidence by two generations", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes network topology: palaces were nodes in a redistribution network that became liability when all faced same exogenous shock. Egypt's geographical and institutional exceptionality explains asymmetrical outcome."}
